| Late 2022 | ~3.00-4.00 | Post-restructuring announcement |
| Early Mar 2023 | ~3.00 | Brief stabilization |
| Mar 16, 2023 | ~1.93 | After SNB facility draw |

~86% decline from Feb 2021 peak (~CHF 14) to March 16 close (~CHF 1.93). The decline accelerated in phases: Archegos (2021), confidence crisis (2022), SVB contagion + Saudi interview (Mar 2023).

label: Credit Suisse suspends $10 billion of funds linked to Greensill Capital
content:
# Credit Suisse suspends $10 billion of funds linked to Greensill Capital

Reuters / Financial Times, March 2021

Credit Suisse has frozen approximately $10 billion in supply chain finance funds connected to Greensill Capital after the specialty finance firm lost its insurance coverage and collapsed into insolvency.

The four funds, marketed under the Credit Suisse Supply Chain Finance Funds banner, invested in notes originated by Greensill Capital, which provided short-term loans to companies based on expected future invoices. Greensill's insurance provider, a subsidiary of Tokio Marine, declined to renew coverage, triggering the firm's rapid unraveling.

Credit Suisse said it had suspended redemptions from the funds "to protect the interests of investors" and would begin liquidating the portfolios. The bank said it was working to recover as much value as possible for investors.

The funds had been marketed to Credit Suisse's wealth management clients as low-risk investments offering stable returns above money market rates. Some clients invested substantial portions of their portfolios in the products.

Lex Greensill, the firm's founder, had maintained close personal relationships with Credit Suisse personnel. Questions have been raised about whether the bank conducted adequate due diligence on Greensill's lending practices, which in some cases involved advancing money against prospective future invoices rather than existing receivables.

The Greensill collapse came in the same month as the Archegos Capital Management debacle, which cost Credit Suisse approximately $5.5 billion. Together, the two events represented over $15 billion in losses or frozen assets in a single month, prompting a comprehensive review of the bank's risk management practices.

Credit Suisse Chairman António Horta-Osório, who was brought in to reform the bank after these twin scandals, himself resigned in January 2022 after an internal investigation found he had violated COVID-19 quarantine rules on multiple occasions.

The Greensill affair led to multiple lawsuits from investors in the funds, ongoing regulatory investigations, and further erosion of Credit Suisse's already damaged reputation.

label: Credit Suisse helped Archegos take 'potentially catastrophic' risks before losing billions when it collapsed
content:
# Credit Suisse helped Archegos take 'potentially catastrophic' risks before losing billions when it collapsed

Source: CNN Business, July 29, 2021 | Axios, July 30, 2021

Credit Suisse published the results of an independent investigation into its $5.5 billion loss related to the collapse of Archegos Capital Management, the family office of Bill Hwang.

The 165-page report prepared by law firm Paul, Weiss, Rifkind, Wharton & Garrison paints a devastating picture of risk management failures.

Key excerpts from the Paul Weiss report:

"The Archegos-related losses sustained by CS are the result of a fundamental failure of management and controls in CS's Investment Bank and, specifically, in its Prime Services business."

"The business was focused on maximizing short-term profits and failed to rein in, and, indeed enabled Archegos's voracious risk-taking."

"There was a persistent failure to escalate and remediate conspicuous risks."

"CS permitted Archegos to assume positions of such size and risk that CS's potential exposure to Archegos was far in excess of the bounds of prudent risk management."

Some numbers from the report:

- Archegos had a net-long bias of more than $7 billion at CS, compared to the next-largest long-biased client at $1.5 billion
- Archegos held a single issuer concentration of $3.3 billion, representing more than 8% of the outstanding free float
- Multiple risk managers flagged concerns. They were ignored or overridden.
- The report describes a "lackadaisical attitude toward risk" in the prime services business
- CS lost approximately $5.5 billion — the largest single loss by any prime broker from the Archegos default
- By comparison, Nomura lost ~$2.9B. Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley exited quickly with minimal losses.

Consequences:
- Several senior executives departed or were fired
- $70 million in executive bonuses clawed back
- CS's prime brokerage reputation was severely damaged
- Board-level governance reforms initiated (later derailed by further scandals)

This was the same month Greensill Capital also collapsed, exposing CS to $10B in frozen supply chain finance funds. Two multi-billion-dollar failures in a single month.

Note: Bill Hwang was later convicted of fraud and market manipulation in connection with Archegos. But CS's losses were fundamentally due to CS's own risk management failures, not Hwang's fraud — other prime brokers with the same client exited with far smaller losses because they had better controls.

label: Credit Suisse shares are a 'steal,' say new Saudi backers after taking 9.9% stake
content:
# Credit Suisse shares are a 'steal,' say new Saudi backers after taking 9.9% stake

CNBC, October 31, 2022

Saudi National Bank, the kingdom's largest lender, has acquired a 9.9% stake in Credit Suisse as part of the Swiss bank's 4 billion Swiss franc ($4.1 billion) capital raise, making it the bank's largest shareholder.

Saudi National Bank CEO Ammar Al Khudairy told CNBC that Credit Suisse shares were "a steal" at the investment price.

"If you look at the franchise value — one of the top 20 investment banks, one of the top 10 wealth managers — actually it's at a discount," Al Khudairy said. "We believe in Credit Suisse's turnaround story."

The Saudi investment was part of a broader restructuring plan announced by Credit Suisse in late October 2022, which included:

- Raising CHF 4 billion in new capital from investors
- Splitting the investment bank, with the advisory business to be rebranded as "First Boston"
- Cutting approximately 9,000 jobs by 2025
- Exiting unprofitable businesses and reducing risk
- Focusing on wealth management and the Swiss domestic bank

Several analysts expressed cautious optimism:

"The capital raise gives CS the runway to execute the restructuring. If they can stabilize deposits and rebuild the investment bank franchise, there's significant upside from current levels."

"The Saudi investment is a vote of confidence from a sophisticated sovereign-adjacent investor. They wouldn't put $1.5 billion in if they didn't believe in the turnaround."

Credit Suisse CEO Ulrich Koerner, who took over in July 2022, said: "We are determined to execute on our comprehensive transformation. This capital raise gives us the resources to do so."

The capital raise was completed in November 2022, raising the full CHF 4 billion despite market skepticism.

However, the Q4 2022 earnings report, released February 9, 2023, revealed CHF 110.5 billion in deposit outflows during the quarter, casting doubt on whether the restructuring plan could be executed quickly enough to stem the bleeding.

label: Credit Suisse Q4 2022 Earnings Release — Capital Structure & Liquidity
content:
# Credit Suisse Q4 2022 Earnings Release — Capital Structure & Liquidity

## Results Summary (verbatim)

"In 4Q22, Credit Suisse reported a net loss attributable to shareholders of CHF 1,393 million, compared to a net loss attributable to shareholders of CHF 2,085 million in 4Q21 and a net loss attributable to shareholders of CHF 4,034 million in 3Q22."

"In 2022, Credit Suisse reported a net loss attributable to shareholders of CHF 7,293 million, which included a valuation allowance in 3Q22 of CHF 3,655 million relating to the reassessment of deferred tax assets as a result of the comprehensive strategic review announced on October 27, 2022, compared to a net loss attributable to shareholders of CHF 1,650 million in 2021."

"In 2022, Credit Suisse reported a loss before taxes of CHF 3,258 million, compared to a loss before taxes of CHF 600 million in 2021. The 2022 results reflected a 34% decrease in net revenues and a 5% decrease in total operating expenses."

label: Credit Suisse Q4 2022 Earnings Release — Capital Structure & Liquidity
content:
# Credit Suisse Q4 2022 Earnings Release — Capital Structure & Liquidity

## Liquidity Issues in 4Q22 (verbatim)

"As previously disclosed, during early 4Q22, Credit Suisse began experiencing significantly higher withdrawals of cash deposits as well as non-renewal of maturing time deposits. However, as the quarter progressed, these outflows stabilized to much lower levels but had not yet reversed by year end, and customer deposits declined by CHF 138 billion in 4Q22."

"As is normal practice, we also limited our access to the capital markets in the period immediately preceding the strategy announcements we made on October 27, 2022. While these outflows led us to partially utilize liquidity buffers at the Group and legal entity level, and we fell below certain legal entity-level regulatory requirements, the core requirements of the liquidity coverage ratio (LCR) and the net stable funding ratio (NSFR) at the Group level were maintained at all times."

"The Group's three-month average daily LCR was 144% as of the end of 4Q22, improved from lower levels earlier in the quarter."

"We issued over USD 5 billion through three bond sales in November and December 2022, which saw strong investor demand, and an additional CHF 4 billion through our capital increases."

label: Credit Suisse Q4 2022 Earnings Release — Capital Structure & Liquidity
content:
# Credit Suisse Q4 2022 Earnings Release — Capital Structure & Liquidity

## Outflows in Assets Under Management (verbatim)

"As previously disclosed, Credit Suisse began experiencing deposit and net asset outflows in early 4Q22 at levels that substantially exceeded the rates incurred in 3Q22. At the Group level, net asset outflows in 4Q22 were approximately 8% of assets under management as of the end of 3Q22, with approximately two-thirds of these net asset outflows in the quarter concentrated in October 2022."

"In Wealth Management, these outflows in 4Q22 had reduced substantially in the rest of the quarter from the elevated levels of early 4Q22, but had not reversed, and represented approximately 15% of assets under management reported as of the end of 3Q22."

"In the Swiss Bank, these outflows in 4Q22 broadly stabilized after the elevated levels of early 4Q22, and represented approximately 2% of assets under management reported as of the end of 3Q22."

label: CREDIT SUISSE GROUP AG FOURTH QUARTER AND FULL YEAR 2022 RESULTS
content:
# CREDIT SUISSE GROUP AG FOURTH QUARTER AND FULL YEAR 2022 RESULTS

Source: SEC Filing, Exhibit 99.1, February 9, 2023

ZURICH, February 9, 2023 – Credit Suisse today reported its 4Q22 and full year 2022 results.

Selected financial highlights (reported):

Fourth quarter 2022:
- Net revenues: CHF 3,057 million
- Provision for credit losses: CHF 78 million
- Total operating expenses: CHF 4,235 million
- Pre-tax loss: CHF (1,391) million
- Net loss attributable to shareholders: CHF (1,393) million

Full year 2022:
- Net revenues: CHF 14,921 million (down from CHF 22,696 million in 2021)
- Net loss attributable to shareholders: CHF (7,293) million
- This compared to a net loss of CHF (1,572) million in 2021

Capital:
- CET1 ratio: 14.1% (vs 14.4% at end of 2021)
- CET1 capital: CHF 36.0 billion
- Tier 1 leverage ratio: 7.7%
- Total capital ratio: approximately 20.0%

THE CRITICAL DISCLOSURE — DEPOSIT OUTFLOWS:

"Net new asset outflows for the Group were CHF (110.5) billion in 4Q22"

This number is buried in the release but it's the single most important data point. CHF 110.5 billion in net outflows in a single quarter. That's roughly a quarter of the deposit base leaving in 90 days.

Context from the earnings call (Motley Fool transcript):

CEO Ulrich Koerner on the earnings call:
"We saw substantial deposit and net asset outflows across our wealth management-related divisions in October, primarily driven by adverse social media coverage and resulting media attention. Outflows moderated as we moved into November and December, and we have started to see net inflows return in January 2023."

"Let me also reiterate that we are determined to execute on our comprehensive transformation."

"The actions we are taking will deliver a simpler, more focused and more stable bank for our clients, shareholders and employees."

CFO Dixit Joshi on capital:
"Our CET1 ratio was 14.1% at year-end and compares favorably to our regulatory requirements and to peers."

Analyst questions included:
- Whether deposit outflows had truly stabilized
- Whether the capital raise would be sufficient
- Whether the restructuring timeline was realistic
- Whether CS could retain key talent during the transformation

Some analysts on the call noted the "disconnect" between the stable capital ratios and the massive deposit flight — essentially asking whether CS was burning through its liquid assets to maintain the appearance of stability.

Other items from the filing:
- Litigation provisions: CHF 690 million for 4Q22
- Restructuring charges: CHF 393 million
- Investment banking revenue: down significantly year over year
- Wealth management: net asset outflows across all regions
- Swiss bank: relatively stable, but not immune

Revenue comparison:
2021: CHF 22,696 million
2022: CHF 14,921 million
That's a 34% revenue decline. The business was shrinking fast.

label: Silicon Valley Bank collapse: How it happened
content:
# Silicon Valley Bank collapse: How it happened

Source: CNBC, March 10, 2023 | FDIC | CNN

Wednesday March 8, 2023:
SVB announced it needed to raise $2.25 billion in capital to shore up its balance sheet. The bank had sold $21 billion in securities from its portfolio at a $1.8 billion loss. The sales were forced by declining deposits as tech startup clients burned through cash.

The announcement spooked investors and depositors. SVB stock began falling in after-hours trading.

Thursday March 9, 2023:
"The run on SVB deposits was stunning in its speed," according to later regulatory reports. Depositors attempted to withdraw approximately $42 billion in a single day — the fastest bank run in history.

VC firms including Peter Thiel's Founders Fund advised portfolio companies to pull deposits from SVB. Word spread through WhatsApp groups and Twitter. The digital bank run was amplified by social media in a way that no previous bank run had been.

SVB stock crashed 60% during the trading session. Trading was halted multiple times.

Friday March 10, 2023:
Before markets opened, the California Department of Financial Protection and Innovation closed SVB and appointed the FDIC as receiver. SVB had $209 billion in total assets and $175.4 billion in deposits at the time of failure.

It was the third-largest bank failure in US history (after Washington Mutual in 2008 and Continental Illinois in 1984 — later surpassed by First Republic in May 2023).

Sunday March 12, 2023:
Signature Bank (New York) also closed by regulators.

Federal government announced extraordinary measures:
- All SVB depositors (including uninsured deposits above $250K) would be made whole
- Federal Reserve created the Bank Term Funding Program (BTFP), allowing banks to borrow against securities at par value rather than market value
- Joint statement from Treasury, Fed, and FDIC: "The U.S. banking system remains resilient and on a solid foundation"

Root cause:
SVB had invested heavily in long-duration bonds and mortgage-backed securities during the 2020-2021 deposit boom. When the Fed raised rates aggressively (0.25% to 4.75% in 12 months), the market value of these holdings dropped dramatically. Unrealized losses exceeded SVB's equity. When depositors withdrew funds faster than SVB could liquidate assets, the bank was done.

Nearly half of US venture capital-backed healthcare and technology companies banked with SVB. It was a concentrated client base that moved as a herd.

Why this matters for CS: SVB's failure had nothing to do with Credit Suisse operationally. Different country, different business model, different problems. But it reignited global banking contagion fears. European bank stocks sold off across the board. And CS — already the weakest major European bank — got hit hardest.

label: Credit Suisse stock and CDS spreads — March 10-14, 2023
content:
# Credit Suisse stock and CDS spreads — March 10-14, 2023

## Credit Suisse (CSGN.SW) — SIX Swiss Exchange

Mar 8: CHF 2.82 (close)
Mar 9: CHF 2.70 (-4.3%) — SVB capital raise announcement spooks markets
Mar 10: CHF 2.52 (-6.7%) — SVB closed by regulators; FDIC appointed receiver
Mar 13: CHF 2.39 (-5.2%) — Monday after Signature Bank closure, global banking selloff
Mar 14: CHF 2.35 (-1.7%) — continued selling but pace slowing

CS was already trading near all-time lows before SVB. Shares had declined from CHF 8+ in early 2022 to under CHF 3 by March 2023.

label: Credit Suisse stock and CDS spreads — March 10-14, 2023
content:
# Credit Suisse stock and CDS spreads — March 10-14, 2023

## CDS spreads (5-year senior)

Credit Default Swap spreads — the annual cost to insure $10 million of CS debt against default:

Early March: ~280-320 basis points
Mar 10: ~380bp
Mar 13: ~420bp
Mar 14: ~450bp

For context, most large European banks were trading at 50-100bp CDS. CS was already 3-4x wider than peers BEFORE the acute crisis.

Deutsche Bank: ~100-120bp
UBS: ~50-70bp
BNP Paribas: ~45-60bp
Barclays: ~60-80bp
Credit Suisse: ~280-450bp

The divergence was already extreme. CS was being priced as a fundamentally different credit risk than its peers. The SVB shock widened everyone, but CS widened proportionally more.

Reuters, March 13: "Credit Suisse credit default swaps jumped to their highest level since the 2008 financial crisis on Monday, as investors grew increasingly worried about the health of the Swiss bank following the collapse of Silicon Valley Bank."

Bloomberg, March 14: "Credit Suisse's five-year credit default swaps climbed to around 450 basis points, signaling deep market anxiety about the bank's future despite management assertions that the restructuring remains on track."

Volume in CS stock traded was approximately 2-3x normal during this period, with heavy selling from institutional investors reducing exposure.

label: Regulatory Stability Statements — March 2023
content:
# Regulatory Stability Statements — March 2023

## US Federal Reserve / FDIC / Treasury Joint Statement (March 12, 2023)

"After receiving a recommendation from the boards of the FDIC and the Federal Reserve, and consulting with the President, Secretary Yellen approved actions enabling the FDIC to complete its resolution of Silicon Valley Bank, Santa Clara, California, in a manner that fully protects all depositors. Depositors will have access to all of their money starting Monday, March 13, 2023. No losses associated with the resolution of Silicon Valley Bank will be borne by the taxpayer."

"We are also announcing a similar systemic risk exception for Signature Bank, New York, New York, which was closed today by its state chartering authority."

"The U.S. banking system remains resilient and on a solid foundation, in large part due to reforms that were made after the financial crisis that ensured better safeguards for the banking industry."

label: Regulatory Stability Statements — March 2023
content:
# Regulatory Stability Statements — March 2023

## ECB Governing Council (March 16, 2023)

The ECB proceeded with its planned 50 basis point rate hike on March 16 — a deliberate signal that it viewed the banking system as stable enough to continue monetary tightening.

ECB press conference excerpt:
President Lagarde: "The euro area banking sector is resilient, with strong capital and liquidity positions. In any case, the ECB's policy toolkit is fully equipped to provide liquidity support to the euro area financial system if needed and to preserve the smooth transmission of monetary policy."

label: Regulatory Stability Statements — March 2023
content:
# Regulatory Stability Statements — March 2023

## SNB/FINMA Joint Statement (March 15, 2023)

Published on the evening of March 15, after CS stock crashed 30%:

"Credit Suisse meets the capital and liquidity requirements imposed on systemically important banks. If necessary, the SNB will provide CS with liquidity."

Full text was brief — just two sentences. The brevity itself is notable. This is the entirety of the formal regulatory reassurance for a G-SIB whose stock just crashed 30%.

label: Regulatory Stability Statements — March 2023
content:
# Regulatory Stability Statements — March 2023

## Bank of England (March 13-14, 2023)

"The UK banking system is well capitalised and funded, and remains safe and sound."

The BoE also facilitated the sale of SVB UK to HSBC for £1, protecting all UK depositors.

label: Regulatory Stability Statements — March 2023
content:
# Regulatory Stability Statements — March 2023

## ECB Banking Supervision (Andrea Enria, March 2023)

"I want to emphasize that the European banking sector has no meaningful exposure to SVB. European banks are fundamentally different from the US regional banks that have been in the headlines."

label: Regulatory Stability Statements — March 2023
content:
# Regulatory Stability Statements — March 2023

## Note on the SNB/FINMA statement specifically:

The SNB/FINMA statement is the most important for the CS case. On its face it's reassuring: the regulators are vouching for CS. But consider:

1. The SNB doesn't issue joint statements about UBS meeting its requirements. Healthy banks don't need public regulatory endorsements.

2. "If necessary, the SNB will provide CS with liquidity" — if CS truly meets requirements, why preemptively offer emergency facilities?

3. The next day (March 16), CS drew CHF 50 billion from the SNB. That's the largest emergency draw in Swiss banking history.

4. Historical comparison: SEC Chairman Chris Cox said Bear Stearns had "a strong capital position" on March 11, 2008. Bear was acquired by JPMorgan on March 16, 2008.

label: "European banks are not US regional banks" — analyst and regulatory consensus, March 2023
content:
# "European banks are not US regional banks" — analyst and regulatory consensus, March 2023

Compiled from multiple sources: Reuters, Bloomberg, ECB, analyst notes — March 10-16, 2023

In the days following Silicon Valley Bank's failure, a chorus of analysts, strategists, and regulators insisted that European banks were fundamentally different from US regional banks and that contagion fears were overblown.

ECB Vice-President Luis de Guindos, March 13: "European banks are in a completely different situation from SVB. They have strong capital and liquidity positions."

Andrea Enria, Chair of ECB Banking Supervision: "I want to emphasize that the European banking sector has no meaningful exposure to SVB. European banks are fundamentally different from the US regional banks that have been in the headlines."

The ECB proceeded with its planned 50 basis point rate hike on March 16, sending a deliberate signal that it viewed the banking system as stable enough to continue monetary tightening.

Stuart Graham, Autonomous Research analyst: "The sell-off in European banks is an overreaction. European banks have been stress-tested repeatedly, hold far more capital than US regionals, and don't have the same interest rate exposure."

JPMorgan European banks research team, March 13: "We see very limited read-across from SVB to European banks. The business models, regulatory frameworks, and interest rate exposures are fundamentally different."

Key structural differences cited:
- European banks subject to full Basel III implementation; US regional banks exempted from many requirements under 2018 Economic Growth Act
- European banks undergo annual EBA/ECB stress tests
- European banks hold higher Liquidity Coverage Ratios (LCR)
- European bank deposit bases are more diversified (not concentrated in one industry)
- European banks have access to ECB lending facilities

Bank of England statement, March 14: "UK banks are well capitalised and funded. The UK banking system remains safe and sound."

Multiple analysts specifically addressed Credit Suisse:

"CS has its own well-documented issues, but they have absolutely nothing to do with SVB. CS's problems are governance and strategy, not interest rate risk. Conflating them is intellectually lazy."

"European banks are not SVB. Credit Suisse is not SVB. The comparison is misleading and is causing unwarranted panic."

"The sell-off in European bank stocks is a buying opportunity. These banks are trading below book value despite strong fundamentals."

The Euro STOXX Banks index (SX7E) fell approximately 16% during March 2023 but subsequently recovered. No European bank other than Credit Suisse required intervention.

label: VIDEO: CS CEO Ulrich Koerner Statements
content:
# VIDEO: CS CEO Ulrich Koerner Statements

## Key appearances

LinkedIn/Internal Video Message (reported March 2023):
Koerner reportedly recorded video messages for CS staff during the crisis week, reassuring employees. Not publicly released but described in media reports.

March 16 Statement (after SNB facility draw):
"This demonstrates decisive action to pre-emptively strengthen Credit Suisse's liquidity. We are taking the necessary steps to protect our clients, our employees, and our stakeholders."

Internal communications (later reported by media) showed Koerner trying to maintain morale while privately acknowledging the severity. Staff were described as demoralized, many updating CVs.

Koerner's public appearances during the crisis week were limited. CS may have calculated that putting Koerner on live TV risked another "absolutely not" moment.

label: Credit Suisse FY2022 Annual Report — Risk Disclosures
content:
# Credit Suisse FY2022 Annual Report — Risk Disclosures

## Deposit Outflows Risk Factor (verbatim from filing)

"Our businesses rely significantly on our deposit base for funding. Our businesses benefit from short-term funding sources, including primarily demand deposits, inter-bank loans, time deposits and cash bonds. Although deposits have been, over time, a stable source of funding, this may not continue, and we may experience, as we did in the fourth quarter of 2022, deposit outflows at levels that substantially exceed rates typically incurred."

"Deposits could also be negatively affected by clients instead choosing to seek deposits or securities products offering higher yields, clients switching to an alternative financial institution which they perceive to be safer or changes in client spending behavior as a result of inflation or other economic developments resulting in an increased need for cash."

label: Credit Suisse FY2022 Annual Report — Risk Disclosures
content:
# Credit Suisse FY2022 Annual Report — Risk Disclosures

## Q4 2022 Outflow Consequences (verbatim from filing)

"As previously disclosed, early in the fourth quarter of 2022, Credit Suisse began experiencing significantly higher withdrawals of cash deposits, non-renewal of maturing time deposits and net asset outflows at levels that substantially exceeded the rates incurred in the third quarter of 2022. These outflows stabilized to much lower levels but had not yet reversed as of the date of this report."

"These outflows led us to partially utilize liquidity buffers at the Group and legal entity level, and we fell below certain legal entity-level regulatory requirements."

"This reduction in assets under management is expected to lead to reduced net interest income and recurring commissions and fees for the Group, which in turn could affect our ability to achieve our capital position objectives."

"A failure to reverse these outflows and to restore our assets under management and deposits could have a material adverse effect on our results of operations and financial condition."

label: Credit Suisse FY2022 Annual Report — Risk Disclosures
content:
# Credit Suisse FY2022 Annual Report — Risk Disclosures

## Credit Rating Downgrades — Complete Timeline (verbatim from filing)

"In May 2022, Standard and Poor's Global Ratings Europe Limited (S&P) lowered its long-term issuer credit ratings, and Fitch Ratings Limited (Fitch) lowered its long-term issuer default ratings of Credit Suisse AG and Credit Suisse Group AG by one notch."

"In August 2022, Moody's Investors Service (Moody's) lowered its long-term senior unsecured debt and deposit ratings of Credit Suisse AG and its senior unsecured debt ratings of Credit Suisse Group AG by one notch, and Fitch lowered its long-term issuer default ratings of Credit Suisse Group AG and the long- and short-term issuer default ratings of Credit Suisse AG, in each case by one notch."

"On November 1, 2022, Moody's downgraded the long-term senior unsecured debt and deposit ratings of Credit Suisse AG by one notch, and also downgraded all the short-term ratings by one notch and maintained the 'negative' outlook on all ratings. S&P downgraded the long-term issuer credit ratings of Credit Suisse Group AG and the long- and short-term issuer credit ratings of Credit Suisse AG, in each case by one notch on November 1, 2022."

Summary: **Five separate downgrade actions across three agencies in 10 months** (May, August, November 2022).

label: Credit Suisse FY2022 Annual Report — Risk Disclosures
content:
# Credit Suisse FY2022 Annual Report — Risk Disclosures

## Impact of Downgrades (verbatim)

"These downgrades in our ratings increasingly elevated our borrowing costs and limited our ability to renew maturing short-term funding and to access short-term funding markets. The downgrades have increased our cost of capital and adversely affected and may in the future continue to adversely affect the ability of our businesses to sell or market their products, engage in business transactions."

label: Credit Suisse FY2022 Annual Report — Risk Disclosures
content:
# Credit Suisse FY2022 Annual Report — Risk Disclosures

## Going Concern Capital Requirements (verbatim from filing)

"Under the Capital Adequacy Ordinance, Swiss banks classified as systemically important banks operating internationally, such as Credit Suisse, are subject to two different minimum requirements for loss-absorbing capacity: such banks must hold sufficient capital that absorbs losses to ensure continuity of service (going concern requirement), and they must issue sufficient debt instruments to fund an orderly resolution without recourse to public resources (gone concern requirement). Going concern capital and gone concern capital together form our total loss-absorbing capacity (TLAC)."

"The going concern requirement for a G-SIB consists of (i) base requirements of 12.86% of RWA and 4.5% of leverage exposure; and (ii) add-ons for market share in the domestic lending and deposit business and for the size of the bank as measured by total exposures (surcharges), which reflect the G-SIB's systemic importance."

"Currently, for Credit Suisse, the going concern surcharge is 0.72% for the RWA ratio and 0.25% for the leverage ratio. For Credit Suisse, this translates into a going concern requirement before any countercyclical buffers of 13.58% of RWA and 4.75% of leverage exposure."

"The going concern requirement for Credit Suisse shall be met with minimum CET1 capital of 9.28% for the RWA ratio and 3.25% for the leverage ratio, with the remainder to be met with a maximum of 4.3% additional tier 1 capital for the RWA ratio and 1.5% for the leverage ratio."

label: Credit Suisse FY2022 Annual Report — Risk Disclosures
content:
# Credit Suisse FY2022 Annual Report — Risk Disclosures

## AT1 Grandfathering (verbatim)

"Under the Capital Adequacy Ordinance's grandfathering provisions, additional tier 1 capital instruments with a low trigger qualify as going concern capital until their first call date."

label: Analyst and Commentator Defenses of Credit Suisse During Crisis Week
content:
# Analyst and Commentator Defenses of Credit Suisse During Crisis Week

Throughout the week of March 13-17, 2023, a number of analysts and market commentators pushed back on the panic surrounding Credit Suisse, arguing the bank's situation was fundamentally different from the US bank failures that had spooked markets.

On the structural comparison to SVB, defenders were emphatic: "Credit Suisse is a globally systemically important bank with a 167-year history. Comparing it to a US regional bank focused on tech startups is absurd." Others pointed out that "CS's problems are governance and strategy, not interest rate risk or asset-liability mismatch. This is a completely different situation from SVB." Credit Suisse's CET1 capital ratio of 14.1% was cited repeatedly as far above regulatory minimums.

On the regulatory side, commentators noted that "FINMA would never let a G-SIB fail. The too-big-to-fail framework exists precisely for this situation." The Swiss government was seen as having every incentive to protect one of only two Swiss G-SIBs. And unlike SVB, CS had access to central bank liquidity facilities — "That's the whole point of being a regulated bank."

There were valuation arguments too. CS was trading below tangible book value. "Even in a worst-case scenario, the franchise has value." Some argued the wealth management business alone was worth more than the current market cap, and that "at these prices, you're getting the entire investment bank for free."

On March 16, the day after his "absolutely not" comments triggered the crash, Saudi National Bank chairman Ammar Al Khudairy attempted to walk back the damage. He told reporters:

> "The panic is unwarranted... the bank is being unfairly targeted."

Sources: CNBC, "Saudi National Bank says panic over Credit Suisse is 'unwarranted'" (March 16, 2023); various analyst notes and media commentary from March 13-17, 2023.

label: Analyst and Commentator Attacks on Credit Suisse During Crisis Week
content:
# Analyst and Commentator Attacks on Credit Suisse During Crisis Week

Not everyone was defending Credit Suisse during the week of March 13-17, 2023. A number of analysts and commentators were blunt about what they saw as a bank in terminal decline.

"Credit Suisse has been a walking dead bank for at least two years. The only question was when, not if." That was a common refrain. Others ticked through the litany: "This is a bank that lost $5.5 billion on Archegos, had $10 billion frozen in Greensill, lost 25% of its deposits in a single quarter, and is now caught in SVB contagion. How many more warnings do you need?" One analyst quipped, "CS has had more restructuring plans than successful quarters."

On governance and culture, the criticism was withering. "Three CEOs in three years. A chairman who resigned over a COVID quarantine breach. Spying on departing executives. This is not a well-run institution." The issue wasn't capital ratios or regulation, critics argued. "The problem is that nobody trusts CS's management to execute anything." As one commentator put it: "Every time CS says they've turned the corner, they walk into another wall."

The deposit flight numbers drew particular attention. CS had reported CHF 110 billion in deposit outflows in a single quarter. "You can't restructure your way out of that," said one banking analyst. Another argued the point more broadly: "Deposits are confidence. When a quarter of your deposit base leaves, confidence is gone. And confidence, once lost in banking, doesn't come back gradually — it comes back never." The Q4 2022 earnings call, said one observer, "showed a bank that is technically solvent but fundamentally broken."

On the SVB contagion angle, critics drew a distinction: "SVB didn't cause CS's problems. SVB exposed CS's problems. CS was already the weakest major bank in Europe." Or as one analyst colorfully put it: "If you're the sick animal in the herd, any loud noise will trigger the predators. SVB was the loud noise."

Credit default swap spreads became their own talking point. "CDS above 500bp for a G-SIB is not a warning sign. It's a death sentence. The market is telling you this bank is done." By March 15 with CDS above 800bp, one trader said: "When CDS hits 800bp, you're not pricing risk anymore. You're pricing certainty of government intervention."

Some analysts went further and predicted specific elements of the resolution. "If FINMA steps in, AT1 bondholders should be very worried. Swiss AT1 bonds have different write-down mechanics than EU bonds." Multiple commentators said the only realistic outcome was a forced merger with UBS — "Nothing else makes sense." One prescient voice noted: "Equity might actually survive this — the question is whether bondholders will."

Sources: Reuters Credit Suisse crisis coverage (March 2023); various analyst notes, opinion pieces, and media commentary; Financial Twitter/X commentary from bank analysts.

label: Saudi National Bank Chairman Interview — Bloomberg TV, March 15, 2023
content:
# Saudi National Bank Chairman Interview — Bloomberg TV, March 15, 2023

## Transcript of key exchange:

BLOOMBERG: Would Saudi National Bank be open to increasing your stake in Credit Suisse, if they were to have another call for fresh money, for instance?

AL KHUDAIRY: The answer is absolutely not, for many reasons outside the simplest reason, which is regulatory and statutory. We now own 9.9% of the bank. If we go above 10%, all kinds of new rules kick in, whether be it by our regulator, or the European regulator, or the Swiss regulator. So that is not something that we would consider.

He continued in the interview expressing confidence in Credit Suisse's management and restructuring plan. He also discussed broader topics including the Fed's rate path, SVB, and the Saudi economy.

When asked if he was concerned about his investment in CS, he said something to the effect of: "We are comfortable with our investment. Credit Suisse has a strong franchise and a solid restructuring plan."

label: Saudi National Bank Chairman Interview — Bloomberg TV, March 15, 2023
content:
# Saudi National Bank Chairman Interview — Bloomberg TV, March 15, 2023

## What the market did:

Credit Suisse stock on SIX Swiss Exchange (CSGN.SW):
- Trading around CHF 2.20 before the interview
- Dropped to as low as CHF 1.55 during the afternoon session
- Closed around CHF 1.70
- Intraday decline: approximately 30% at the worst point
- Trading volume exploded — over 500 million shares changed hands (normal is ~30M)

CS 5-year CDS: jumped from approximately 500 basis points to over 800 basis points during the session, briefly touching 1,000bp.

European bank index (SX7E): fell 3-5% in sympathy.

label: Saudi National Bank Chairman Interview — Bloomberg TV, March 15, 2023
content:
# Saudi National Bank Chairman Interview — Bloomberg TV, March 15, 2023

## From CNBC reporting (March 16, 2023):

"Saudi National Bank says panic over Credit Suisse is 'unwarranted'"

The day AFTER the interview, Al Khudairy tried to calm things:
"The panic in the market is a little bit unwarranted. We've invested at a price that was attractive for us. We're comfortable with our position."

He also said he maintained regular contact with CS leadership and was "satisfied with the turnaround plan."

label: Saudi National Bank Chairman Interview — Bloomberg TV, March 15, 2023
content:
# Saudi National Bank Chairman Interview — Bloomberg TV, March 15, 2023

## From CNN Business (March 15, 2023):

"Credit Suisse borrows more than $50 billion from Swiss National Bank after shares crash 30%"

"Shares of Credit Suisse sank over 30% after Saudi National Bank chairman Ammar Al Khudairy told Bloomberg Television that its biggest shareholder would not provide more money to the Swiss lender, citing regulatory constraints."

"The plunge deepened a week of turmoil for European banks, which have fallen sharply since the sudden collapse of SVB Financial."

label: Saudi National Bank Chairman Interview — Bloomberg TV, March 15, 2023
content:
# Saudi National Bank Chairman Interview — Bloomberg TV, March 15, 2023

## Aftermath:

Al Khudairy resigned as Saudi National Bank chairman on March 27, 2023, citing "personal reasons." He had been chairman since the bank's formation through the merger of National Commercial Bank and Samba Financial Group in 2021.

Saudi National Bank lost over $1 billion on its Credit Suisse investment (invested CHF 1.4B, received approximately CHF 280M worth of UBS shares at the merger price of CHF 0.76).

label: VIDEO: Saudi National Bank Chairman Bloomberg TV Interview
content:
# VIDEO: Saudi National Bank Chairman Bloomberg TV Interview

## Transcript (key exchange)

BLOOMBERG INTERVIEWER: Would Saudi National Bank be open to increasing your stake in Credit Suisse?

AMMAR AL KHUDAIRY: The answer is absolutely not, for many reasons outside the simplest reason, which is regulatory and statutory. If we go above 10%, all kinds of new rules kick in, whether be it by our regulator, or the European regulator, or the Swiss regulator. So that is not something that we would consider.

He continued to discuss the bank's investment thesis, expressing confidence in Credit Suisse's restructuring plan and describing the panic as "unwarranted."

label: VIDEO: Saudi National Bank Chairman Bloomberg TV Interview
content:
# VIDEO: Saudi National Bank Chairman Bloomberg TV Interview

## Visual/Audio observations:

- Al Khudairy appeared calm and matter-of-fact when delivering the "absolutely not"
- His tone suggested he was stating something obvious and routine
- No visible signs of panic or concern — relaxed, conversational body language
- He seemed genuinely surprised by the market's interpretation

label: VIDEO: Saudi National Bank Chairman Bloomberg TV Interview
content:
# VIDEO: Saudi National Bank Chairman Bloomberg TV Interview

## Real-time market reaction (visible on-screen during the interview):

- CS stock began dropping immediately as the headline crossed
- Bloomberg's own ticker showed the real-time crash
- Within minutes of the "absolutely not" quote, CS was down 10%+
- By end of day: -30% intraday, close at CHF 1.70
- CDS exploded above 800bp

label: SNB and FINMA Issue Joint Statement on Credit Suisse
content:
# SNB and FINMA Issue Joint Statement on Credit Suisse

Following the 30% crash in Credit Suisse shares on March 15, 2023, the Swiss National Bank and the Swiss Financial Market Supervisory Authority FINMA took the unusual step of issuing a joint public statement on the bank's condition.

The key line from the statement:

> "Credit Suisse meets the capital and liquidity requirements imposed on systemically important banks."

The SNB further stated it would provide Credit Suisse with liquidity "if necessary," signaling that emergency central bank facilities were available to the bank.

Both the central bank and the regulator were vouching specifically for CS's compliance with regulatory requirements — not a vague "we're monitoring the situation" but a direct claim that the bank met its capital and liquidity thresholds. CS's CET1 ratio stood at 14.1% versus an approximate 10% regulatory minimum.

Joint central bank-regulator statements of this kind are rare. The SNB does not issue public endorsements of banks meeting their requirements under normal circumstances.

For context, similar reassurances have been issued at other institutions in the past. SEC Chairman Chris Cox said Bear Stearns had "a strong capital position" on March 11, 2008; Bear was acquired by JPMorgan on March 16. Treasury Secretary Paulson expressed confidence in Lehman Brothers' capital in August 2008; Lehman filed for bankruptcy on September 15. The Bank of England said Northern Rock was "solvent" in September 2007; it was nationalized in February 2008.

In the days following the SNB/FINMA statement, events moved fast. On March 16, Credit Suisse drew CHF 50 billion from the SNB's emergency liquidity facility. On March 17, CS stock fell another 8% despite the liquidity draw. On March 19, UBS acquired Credit Suisse at CHF 0.76 per share, and AT1 bonds were written down to zero.

The joint statement bought Credit Suisse approximately 96 hours.

Sources: SNB Press Release (March 15, 2023); FINMA Communications (March 15, 2023).

label: Credit Suisse to borrow up to CHF50 billion from Swiss National Bank
content:
# Credit Suisse to borrow up to CHF50 billion from Swiss National Bank

SWI swissinfo.ch
Published: March 16, 2023 - 17:54

Switzerland's second largest bank announced plans to borrow up to CHF50 billion ($54 billion) from the Swiss central bank to strengthen its liquidity position following market turbulence triggered by bank failures in the United States.

"This additional liquidity would support Credit Suisse's core businesses and clients as Credit Suisse takes the necessary steps to create a simpler and more focused bank," the institution stated in a Wednesday evening announcement.

The bank also committed to accessing a short-term liquidity facility and repurchasing approximately CHF3 billion in debt.

Market reaction proved initially positive. Credit Suisse shares surged as much as 32% when trading opened Thursday, though gains moderated during the session, closing up 19% after the previous day's devastating 25% collapse.

The Swiss National Bank (SNB) and Swiss Financial Market Supervisory Authority (FINMA) released a joint statement Wednesday evening confirming readiness to provide emergency liquidity support if needed. Their statement emphasized that "problems of certain banks in the USA do not pose a direct risk of contagion for the Swiss financial markets."

This intervention demonstrated authorities' commitment to preventing collapse of a systemically important institution. The move aimed to reassure depositors and prevent potential bank runs.

Credit Suisse's largest shareholder, Saudi National Bank, had declined further capital injections. Major investor Harris Associates simultaneously announced its exit from the bank.

FINMA confirmed Credit Suisse maintained adequate capital and liquidity reserves for systemically important banks, suggesting regulatory confidence in the institution's stability despite recent turmoil.

---

From Banking Dive, March 16, 2023:

"Credit Suisse borrows $54B from Swiss central bank"

Credit Suisse said Thursday it would draw up to 50 billion Swiss francs ($54 billion) from the Swiss National Bank under a covered loan facility and a short-term liquidity facility, as the troubled lender seeks to ease investor fears and prevent a liquidity crisis.

The move came after the bank's shares plummeted 24% on Wednesday following comments by the chairman of its largest investor, Saudi National Bank, saying his institution would "absolutely not" add to its roughly 10% stake.

The Swiss National Bank said it was "providing liquidity" to Credit Suisse in a brief statement Thursday.

Despite the lifeline, Credit Suisse shares fell sharply again Friday — dropping another 8% — suggesting the emergency borrowing had not fully calmed nerves.

label: Thomas Jordan: The Swiss National Bank's role as lender of last resort in the crisis at Credit Suisse
content:
# Thomas Jordan: The Swiss National Bank's role as lender of last resort in the crisis at Credit Suisse

Summary of the keynote speech by Mr Thomas Jordan, Chairman of the Governing Board of the Swiss National Bank, at the conference "The SNB and its Watchers", Berne, 1 November 2023.

---

The crisis at Credit Suisse has opened up a broad debate on the financial stability framework in Switzerland. The National Bank Act states that the Swiss National Bank shall contribute to the stability of the financial system, and legislators have allocated certain tasks and instruments for this purpose. In the event of a crisis, the SNB performs this mandate in particular by acting as lender of last resort and providing liquidity to systemically important domestic banks that are solvent but can no longer refinance themselves on the market. In March 2023, the crisis at Credit Suisse placed heavy demands on the SNB in its role as lender of last resort. By making liquidity assistance available on an unprecedented scale, the SNB first created the time window necessary to find a solution for Credit Suisse, and thereafter provided significant support in the acquisition of Credit Suisse by UBS.

The acquisition of Credit Suisse by UBS on 19 March this year prevented a global financial crisis. Since autumn 2022, the authorities had also examined options other than an acquisition. These included a resolution of Credit Suisse or a temporary public ownership (TPO) of the bank. After considering the risks involved, the authorities ultimately came out in favour of the acquisition of Credit Suisse by UBS. Given the fragility of the financial markets at that time, there were fears that a resolution would destabilise the financial system. In the case of a TPO, the risks for taxpayers were considered too high.

The SNB's liquidity assistance was decisive in the successful handling of the crisis. Never before had a central bank provided such a large amount of liquidity to a single bank. At its peak, the total was CHF 168 billion in three different currencies, which the SNB made available to Credit Suisse on the same days the liquidity was requested. It was only thanks to its well-established processes and good preparation for crisis scenarios that the SNB was able to provide Credit Suisse with the necessary liquidity in such a short period of time, thus enabling the bank to meet its obligations in full when the markets opened.

Providing liquidity assistance to Credit Suisse required the utmost flexibility from the SNB. As lender of last resort, it initially provided Credit Suisse with classic liquidity assistance (ELA). Based on an emergency ordinance by the Federal Council, it also provided an additional liquidity assistance loan (ELA+). The statutory basis for this facility made it possible for loans granted under ELA+ to be secured solely by means of preferential rights in bankruptcy proceedings. Ultimately the SNB granted a further loan guaranteed by the federal government's Public Liquidity Backstop (PLB).

Thanks to ELA+, it was possible to create the time window required for finding a comprehensive solution during the phase of high and exceptionally rapid outflows at Credit Suisse. Without ELA+ Credit Suisse would have been in jeopardy of being unable to meet its financial obligations before the announcement of the acquisition by UBS, entailing substantial risks for financial stability. However, even after the acquisition had been announced, the SNB's liquidity assistance remained crucial for Credit Suisse and UBS.

There are statutory limits on the SNB's room for manoeuvre in the area of financial stability. Further tasks that are also important for the stability of the financial system fall under the responsibility of different authorities. For example, the SNB is not responsible for the supervision of banks, and it has no legal basis to take over or resolve a bank. Similarly, the SNB cannot guarantee customer deposits at banks or provide banks with unsecured liquidity, i.e. without sufficient collateral cover -- also not in the case of an acute crisis that poses a threat to global financial stability. The statutory framework for the SNB's tasks and instruments is clear, and so too is the division of roles between the authorities.

For the SNB, its role as lender of last resort is of the utmost importance. Neither in the case of the crisis at Credit Suisse nor in preceding crises was there ever a situation in which the SNB would not have provided emergency liquidity assistance if this had been requested by a bank or if necessary to safeguard financial stability.

The SNB works continuously on being able to optimally perform its tasks as lender of last resort at any time. This includes testing its own emergency operations, as well as maintaining good contacts with other key central banks, relationships that are particularly valuable when it comes to providing liquidity assistance in foreign currency in crisis situations. However, it also involves regularly reviewing its framework for eligible collateral and engaging in dialogue with other central banks in this regard.

The SNB accepts a broad range of collateral that banks can provide in return for liquidity. This includes mortgages both on residential and on commercial properties. These constitute more than 85% of the domestic credit volume of Swiss banks. The SNB also accepts as collateral securities with lower credit ratings, loan securitisations and shares. The collateral can also originate from borrowers outside Switzerland and be denominated in foreign currencies. In terms of the range, quality and risk-based haircuts, the collateral deemed eligible by the SNB is comparable to that of other central banks.

Furthermore, the SNB is continuously developing its collateral framework. For example, in 2019 it initiated a project to ensure that all banks, not just those designated as being systemically important, have access to liquidity against mortgages. Following the conclusion of a pilot phase, banks can obtain liquidity provided they have made the corresponding preparations.

What lessons have been learned from this crisis with regard to the provision of emergency liquidity? The case of Credit Suisse has clearly shown that outflows of customer deposits can now be much faster and more extensive than assumed by the existing regulations. Moreover, Credit Suisse had not prepared sufficient assets that it could provide to the SNB as collateral in order to access massive amounts of emergency liquidity assistance in a crisis. That is why emergency law had to be used to make ELA+ possible.

The following lessons can be learned from this: First, the liquidity regulations must be geared to the new reality of potentially faster and larger outflows of deposits. Second, it is of the utmost importance going forward that banks prepare sufficient collateral that they can transfer to the SNB and other central banks. Third, there needs to be an effective PLB that enables the SNB to provide liquidity loans to banks in difficulties that do not have sufficient collateral. The risks of the PLB are borne by the state. This approach tallies with the established allocation of roles between state and central bank in a banking crisis. ELA+ should not become part of the SNB's regular set of instruments.

The SNB's willingness and ability to provide liquidity was crucial in managing the acute crisis at Credit Suisse and thus in avoiding a financial crisis with serious economic consequences for Switzerland and the rest of the world. Even in an acute crisis situation, however, the SNB must always adhere to the framework established by law and comply with the allocation of roles to authorities envisaged therein.
